paragraphs.body: I never understood why I was given this ability. It isn’t something I became fully conscious of until I was about eight or nine anyway. Before then I just remember feeling deeply confused for brief amounts of time when the world around me would come to a complete halt and I alone was able to act and speak freely. Time would always then continue on without anyone batting an eye. When I was younger I never felt that it was necessarily a blessing or a curse, but rather a skill that’s been impossible to perfect. I’ve spent some years using these frozen minutes to my advantage and some years using the time to do things I soon regretted after. Some years I simply sat with myself in the stillness, too paralyzed by stress over whatever implications my actions would have if I chose to interact with the world around me.

annotations.comment: I like being in the mind of this narrator — what utter confusion they must feel! I did think 8 or 9 was pretty old to have realized what was happening (and I suspect that they vividly remember the day of that realization).

paragraphs.body: On my tenth birthday, I used my ten minutes while my mom and I were in the checkout line to run back to the doll section of Target so I could sneak more clothes and accessories into our cart before we reached the front of the check out line.

annotations.comment: This is such a great example of what a 10 year old would do with this ability. Reword to avoid saying “check out line” twice.

paragraphs.body: On my sixteenth birthday I used my sixteen minutes to go through my old friend Luna’s text messages with Claire to figure out why Claire hadn’t come to my birthday party. Turns out Claire bailed on me for a guy that night instead and Amelia didn’t have the guts to tell me. Neither of them were invited to another birthday party after that.

annotations.comment: Again, a great example and again, the wording is a little unclear.

paragraphs.body: On my eighteenth birthday, I used my eighteen minutes right before I left the house at 9:30PM to bust open my parents liquor cabinet and fill up a nondescript water bottle with whatever vodka I could find. I then ran it outside and threw the bottle in a bush where my parents wouldn’t notice me digging for it when my friends came to pick me up for my party that night. After accomplishing what I hoped to in my allotted minutes I, same as every year, resumed whatever position I was in before deciding to press pause. This time it was sitting at the island in my kitchen waiting for my parents to finish their lecture on partying.

annotations.comment: This made me laugh. This action surely took less than 18 minutes, though?

paragraphs.body: “Have fun, but be smart.”
“Don’t be that person.”
“Be home by 1.”
Etc, etc, etc.

Now, in the middle of my twenty-fifth birthday, I sit here racking my brain over when in the rest of my night I’ll choose to put the world on pause for twenty five minutes. It’s a decision that has gotten increasingly harder for me as the years go on. Years ago I almost always knew right before midnight what to do with this time, when to do it, and why, but it was always to do small childish things. I feared that if I didn’t start using this time for something worthwhile then it would continue to be a waste, but I never understood why I must be the one to feel burdened by this. It’s like being given a key with no doors to open. Twenty five minutes was such an odd length of time. Not sufficient enough to do anything that mattered from my apartment, but too long to be left entirely alone with my thoughts and actions.

annotations.comment: I would like just a little bit on clarity on how this works. She gets the same amount as her birthday, always ON her birthday? But she gets to choose when to use them? And they have to be used concurrently? (Not, like, 3 minutes here and 10 minutes elsewhere?)

paragraphs.body: “Charlie!”

I quickly snap out of my downward spiral looking around the bodies that fill my living room and up at the girl who called my name. My best friend Sarah stands in our kitchen with her head poking around the corner.
“Are you ready?” She asks with excitement.

I nod back with apprehension. She aggressively flicks the lights off and makes her way toward me with what looks to be a poorly homemade, but made with love, birthday cake. It’s lined with sparkler candles and regular wax candles that I’m sure she almost burned the house down trying to light from how much wine she's had. The room fills with the voices of about thirty other friends singing happy birthday to me, each person taking a different pitch or cadence. I give my friends a glowing smile as the last line of the song is practically belted at me and the cake is set in front of my face.

annotations.comment: Nice capturing of this moment. By the way, I appreciated that she feels she has to make better use of her extra minutes now.

paragraphs.body: Sarah sinks into the couch next to me, handing me another glass of cheap pinot grigio.
“Okay, okay, okay, make your wish, and keep drinking!”

I stare down at the cake in front of me, wishing I could wish for something nobody in this room understands. The ability to know what to do with extra time that would actually mean something for once. I try to avoid thinking about the people in this world that probably beg for extra time as they reach the end of their own.

The sparklers continue burning down to their base. A reminder of how long I have left to make a decision. Rather than keeping the crowd waiting, I close my eyes, puff up my cheeks, and pretend I’m making the wish of a lifetime as I dramatically blow out the candles. I laugh to myself before realizing that nobody is clapping for me.

annotations.text: A reminder of how long I have
annotations.comment: Again, a little clarity here would help.

paragraphs.body: I open my eyes. Nobody is clapping for me, because nobody is moving. This has never happened before. I have always had complete and total control over when I get to pause and take my minutes. I never have to say any magical words or anything I just think about it and it happens, almost like breathing. This time; however, I had zero intention of this happening.

annotations.comment: Did this happen when she was a kid, though? Before she realized?
annotations.context_right: over when I get to p

annotations.comment: Great explanation — I would just move it earlier.

paragraphs.body: My mind begins to race not just from the anxiety of how or why my world has stopped, but more so from the anxiety of the fact that my twenty five minutes are now ticking down.

annotations.comment: It might be fun to have her have a plan she intends to follow through on and now need to adjust that.

paragraphs.body: Taking in my surroundings, I try to collect my thoughts and see if there is anything I can do in the current moment. My first instinct is to look to my right at Sarah as if there is anything she can do for me. What I can do for her is take the glass out of her hand and set it back on the coffee table. I look at her again wondering why I’ve never told her. She’s been my best friend since the start of college and it was never something I felt I could bring up to anyone including her out of fear of being called crazy or delusional amongst other things.

I continue looking around at the room of friends I’ve grown to love so dearly who don’t know about this part of my life. Everyone has a smile on their face and is more concerned about my happiness tonight than their own. Meanwhile I’ve been worried sick the past several days over twenty five minutes I fear will determine the rest of my life or amount to insignificance. Over the years, I’ve become increasingly aware of the fact that nobody stresses over their birthday nearly as much as I have grown to. I think this is something I’ve wanted to let go of for a long time now I just haven’t known how. I’ve left behind claw marks hoping that maybe this year will be the year that I finally figure it out and I know I’ll do the same next year and the year after till it exhausts me.

Part of me begins to believe that my paused time taking effect on its own this year has forced me into making this decision. Birthday wishes have always been an odd concept to me, but I also can’t say I’ve ever truly believed what I have wished for in the past will come true.

Looking back at my lopsided cake, I close my eyes once again and take in a deep breath wishing to be set free from the mental battle I find myself in time and time again. I exhale and make a fervorous attempt at blowing out the frozen candles willing myself to move through the rest of the night and the years to come without putting a moment on pause ever again.

annotations.comment: Elizabeth, this is a great concept for your story. I really enjoyed the voice and the idea here. It’s fun to take what seems like a cool concept and think about how it would really play out to have to deal with. I didn’t quite follow your ending. I liked that it was small scale (and maybe you could have her have some big plan that she doesn’t get to pursue, for contrast) but I wasn’t entirely sure what she learned or got by the end. It seemed like the idea of a birthday wish was kind of commingled with having those minutes to herself, but the idea that you always get what you wish for hadn’t come up before. Or was she just wishing without knowing she would get what she wished for? I wasn’t clear. It might be helpful to give another example of a time when the extra minutes were a burden instead of a curse — the incident at her party with her friends’ texts was a good one — and to clarify earlier that she gets to choose when to have the minutes ahead of time (which I was a little confused by — did she magically know her friends wasn’t going to show up so wished for the minutes then?). Super fun. Just needs some thinking through.
